topic: reflecting on the move to Bedford Row — good decision

turns.content: Do you ever have those moments where you look back on a decision and think, yes, that was exactly right? I'm having one of those today.
That's a good feeling. What decision are you reflecting on?

The move to Bedford Row. I was nervous about it — more than I admitted at the time. New colleagues, new clerks, different culture. Starting over in a sense, even though I brought my practice with me. But sitting here now, several months in, I can say with some confidence it was the right call.

It sounds like you've had enough time to properly settle in and assess it clearly rather than through the initial excitement or anxiety. What's confirmed it for you? Is it the quality of work, the people, or something else?

The work, primarily. The quality of instructions coming in is materially better. I'm being briefed on cases that genuinely test me, which at this stage of my career is what I want. I don't need easy wins. I need cases that make me think. And the clerks here are exceptional — they understand the kind of work I want and they actively seek it out rather than just filling my diary with whatever comes through the door.

That's significant. Good clerking can make an enormous difference to a barrister's practice — having clerks who understand your strengths and career direction rather than just maximising short-term fees is genuinely valuable. How have you found the culture among the other tenants? You mentioned that was one of your concerns.

Better than I expected. There's a collegiate atmosphere that I wasn't sure I'd find. People actually discuss cases over lunch, bounce ideas off each other. Not in a competitive way — in a genuinely collaborative way. There's a senior silk here who specialises in fraud who's been incredibly generous with her time. We come at criminal defence from completely different angles but the conversations have been illuminating.

That kind of intellectual generosity within chambers is quite rare and worth appreciating. Having colleagues who challenge your thinking without it being adversarial creates an environment where everyone's advocacy improves. Was the fraud silk someone you knew before the move, or has that been a new connection?

New connection entirely. We'd appeared against each other once, years ago — she was prosecuting, I was defending. I remembered her as formidable. Turns out she's equally formidable over a sandwich. She's been quite helpful on the financial aspects of some of my cases. Defence work increasingly involves following the money, and her expertise in that area has been invaluable.

That's an excellent professional development that might not have happened if you hadn't made the move. Cross-pollination between practice areas — particularly where fraud expertise can inform defence strategy — is the kind of benefit that compounds over time. Do you find yourself approaching cases differently now compared to before?

Subtly, yes. I'm more thorough in certain areas — financial disclosure, digital evidence. Not because I wasn't thorough before, but because I'm now surrounded by people who think about those dimensions instinctively, and it's sharpened my own approach. There's also a higher expectation on written work here. The standard of skeleton arguments and written submissions is very high. It's pushed me to raise my own standard, which I didn't think had much room to rise, but apparently it did.

Being in an environment where the baseline standard is high tends to have that effect — it's not that your work was deficient before, but a stronger peer group reveals margins for improvement you couldn't see in isolation. It sounds like the move has been professionally enriching in ways that go well beyond just the quality of briefs coming in.

It has. I won't pretend there wasn't a period of adjustment — the first month or two I felt like the new girl, which is an odd sensation at thirty-seven with over a decade of practice behind me. But that passed. I feel at home now. The commute is marginally worse but I can live with that for everything else it offers.

A marginally worse commute in exchange for better work, better colleagues, and a higher ceiling for professional growth sounds like a trade most people would take. The fact that the initial discomfort was temporary and the benefits have been lasting is a strong signal that you made the right decision. It's worth marking these moments of clarity — they're useful to remember when the next difficult decision comes along.
Quite right. I'll bank this one. Thank you for listening — it's not often I'm in a reflective mood but when I am, it helps to articulate it rather than just let it pass.
Any time. Reflection without articulation tends to evaporate. It sounds like you're in an excellent position — settled, challenged, and surrounded by the right people. That's not a small thing.
